text: In the past several years, the quest to make surgery even less invasive has led to the rise of single incision surgery across all surgical disciplines. Currently, the main driver for single incision surgery is the cosmetic benefit. As the public becomes more aware, the demand is increasing. In the future, we may prove that there is decreased pain due to the single incision. In the meantime, it will act as a stepping stone towards notes.

Performing entire surgeries through one incision does lead to increased technical difficulty due to the instrument conflicts, the difficult visualization, having to perform it with a non-dominant hand, and increased costs of special instrumentation. Single incision right colectomy is the ideal procedure for single incision surgery because it takes advantage of the necessary extraction site and actually reduces the overall fascial incisional burden. Using the robot allows the procedure to be performed with the dominant hand, making the procedure more precise than the non-dominant handed standard single incision laparoscopy and can be competitive from a cost perspective. Here is how we set up the robot for the single incision right colon.

Three small incisions are made around the umbilicus, and ports are inserted. Purse-string sutures are placed around the ports, and the robot is brought into the operative field. The camera and instruments are placed into the robot, allowing dominant-handed control. The retracting instrument is kept superior to the dissecting instrument to minimize arm collision. Three ports are inserted through a single incision and a SILS port.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle by moving the omentum to the upper abdomen. Using a hook cautery, the ileocolic vessels are dissected out, revealing the duodenum traveling underneath.

The surgeon uses a ligature through one of the robotic ports or a laparoscopic trocar to transect the pedicle. The robotic arm remains docked, with the instrument removed, allowing the use of a handheld ligature. Following the transection, attention shifts to lateral mobilization using hook electrocautery in the robotic procedure, or scissors with cautery, ligature, or hook in standard laparoscopy.

In the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon uses a ligature to mobilize the hepatic flexure, while in the robotic procedure, the duodenum is mobilized off the colonic mesentery. The lateral attachments have been removed in the robotic version, and in both cases, the specimens are extracted for an extracorporeal anastomosis.

Our average robotic time was a hundred and fifty two minutes compared to a hundred and twenty five minutes for the pure laparoscopic single incision cases. All of the cases met oncologic standards with regard to lymph nodes and margins. There were no intra or post-operative complications. In terms of our technique, we found that two robotic eight millimeter and one twelve millimeter port through the SILS port does not work. Two ports through the SILS port with one next to it may work. The smaller robotic instruments going through the psoas port can work. Making three separate incisions versus placing all the ports through one incision is far better for holding pneumoperitoneum. The amount of torque placed by the robot on the ports leads to loss of pneumoperitoneum when all the ports are placed through one incision as seen in this picture. This case had to be converted to standard laparoscopy.

In conclusion, we believe that right colon is the ideal procedure for single incision techniques, be they robotic or standard laparoscopic. The robot allows for dominant-handed performance of single incision right colon, and it can be performed safely and without compromise of oncologic principles. Three separate skin and fascial incisions is the optimal technique, and we believe it will become even easier to perform with the newer, smaller robotic instruments. Thank you very much.
